title: Circularity in Perovskite-Based Tandem Photovoltaics for Terawatt-Scale Deployment
authors: Abderrahime Sekkat, Shiling Dong, Jenny Baker, Matt Burnell, Tapas Mallick, Ruy S. Bonilla, Robert L. Z. Hoye
abstract: As photovoltaics (PVs) scale from one to multiple terawatts over the next decade, ensuring sustainable deployment is urgently required. Crystalline silicon (c-Si) PVs, the current industry standard, will generate an estimated 160 million tonnes of waste by 2050, and there remains complex technoeconomic challenges associated with their recycling. Metal-halide perovskite (MHP)-based tandem PVs not only promise higher power conversion efficiencies than single-junction c-Si devices, but also offer intrinsic advantages for circularity, including simpler device architectures, low-temperature processing, and more accessible materials recovery routes. At this pivotal juncture when perovskite PVs begin to enter the market, this review examines the critical circularity challenges that must be addressed: substitution of scarce raw materials, scalable recycling protocols, cost-effective stack delamination, safe lead sequestration, and policy frameworks to encourage circularity across the device lifecycle with effective incentives. By integrating the materials, technoeconomic and policy dimensions that go beyond conventional lifecycle assessments, we outline actionable strategies to co-optimize device performance and sustainability. This review aims to guide researchers, policymakers, and industry stakeholders in steering perovskite-based tandem PVs towards a circular and responsible commercialization pathway within the global clean-energy transition.
summary: This review examines how perovskite-based tandem solar cells can be deployed sustainably at a global scale. It highlights the potential for lower carbon footprints and better recycling compared to silicon, while warning of risks related to indium scarcity and lead management.
main_problem: The paper addresses the sustainability and circularity challenges of scaling perovskite-based tandem photovoltaics to terawatt-scale deployment, specifically focusing on waste management, material scarcity, and end-of-life recycling.
main_result: The review identifies that while perovskite tandems offer lower carbon footprints and energy payback times than silicon, their success depends on overcoming indium supply risks, managing lead toxicity, and implementing effective recycling protocols for monolithic structures.
method: An integrated review and assessment combining life-cycle assessment (LCA) data, technoeconomic analysis, and policy framework evaluation across the device lifecycle.
model_or_system: The physical systems studied are metal-halide perovskite (MHP) tandem solar cells, including MHP/Silicon (MHP/Si) and all-perovskite architectures, compared against standard crystalline silicon (c-Si) photovoltaics.
key_observables: Power conversion efficiency (PCE), energy payback time (EPBT), global warming potential (GWP), carbon footprint (CO2eq), and Demand-Production Ratio (DPR) for critical materials.
important_parameters: Terawatt-scale deployment, efficiency records (up to 34.85%), and material mass inventories (e.g., Pb content < 0.003 g Wp^-1).
main_assumptions: Assumes the continued scaling of PV capacity to terawatt levels and relies on existing/projected life-cycle assessment models for environmental impact estimation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows projected global PV waste volumes and capacity growth; other figures (inferred) likely illustrate the supply-demand context for indium and the stages of the PV lifecycle.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the sustainability crisis of silicon PV, then evaluates the technical advantages and circularity challenges of perovskite tandems, specifically addressing material substitution, recycling protocols, and policy frameworks needed for responsible commercialization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 | Waste from terawatt-scale PV deployment. a, Estimated cumulative global waste volumes of end-of-life PV modules and PV capacity. Adapted with permission from Ref. 29. b, Global cumulative waste produced from 2016 to 2050, including municipal waste, coal ash, plastic waster, e-waster, oily sludge, and best-/worst-case scenario PV water estimates from modules, and c, diagrammatic representation of the structure composition of a silicon solar panel and solar cell. The typical weight fraction of each component in the c-Si module is suggested. Adapted with permission from Ref. 30.

Fig. 3 | Overview of the TCE landscape for tandem solar cells. a, Market distribution of major TCE materials, illustrating the relative commercial share of ITO (on glass and PET), Ag nanowires, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), conducting polymers (CPs), and other emerging alternatives122. b, Comparison of major classes of TCE materials based on their cost and conductivity. Data shown in Supplementary Table 2. c, Radar diagrams comparing the performance of transparent conducting materials (TCMs) relative to ITO across key criteria, including conductivity, cost, transmittance, stability, scalability, and compatibility with perovskite processing. The detailed data used for these radar diagrams are...

Fig. 4 | Recycling metal-halide perovskite (MHP)-based tandem solar panels. Current recycling is constrained by limited recovery of high-value materials, economic imbalance, public concern over lead, and fragmented regulation. Future circular deployment will require closed-loop material recovery, regenerative device design, economically viable recycling, social acceptance of reuse, and coordinated policy frameworks including extended producer responsibility and eco-design.
